---
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{fvextra}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \DefineVerbatimEnvironment{Highlighting}{Verbatim}{breaklines,commandchars=\\\{\}}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

# Chicago Crime Patterns and Socioeconomic Correlates

| **Group Members** | Xinying Jiang, Henry Liu | **Lecture Section** | Section 4 | **GitHub** | xinying0301, henryliu1125 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## Research Question

This project aims to inform the targeting of public safety and social investment resources, identifying which neighborhoods and time periods need priority intervention. Specifically, it asks two questions: (1) What are the temporal, spatial, and categorical patterns of crime across Chicago census tracts in 2024, and (2) to what extent are neighborhood crime rates associated with socioeconomic conditions (including education attainment, unemployment, and median income)? 

Data sources include the City of Chicago's crime incident dataset (filtered to 2024, with 256,652 observations) and the 2024 American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates (ACS) for socioeconomic variables.

## Approach and Coding

The analysis was organized in four stages. 

First, \textbf{data cleaning}: The 31 original crime primary types were collapsed into four categories (Violent, Property, Regulatory, Other) using a custom classification function. ACS tables required extracting 11-digit GEOID tract codes and computing derived rates (e.g., bachelor+ rate = sum of bachelor's through doctorate holders divided by total population 25+).

Second, \textbf{spatial joining}: The city boundary shapefile clipped tracts to Chicago, removing partial Cook County tracts at the city edge. A GeoPandas `sjoin` with predicate `"within"` assigned each crime location to its census tract, also subseting socioeconomic variables only within Chicago.  Crime rate was calculated as the crime count in each tract divided by the ACS population, scaled per 100 residents.

Third, \textbf{static visualization}: Produce two temporal heatmaps, two choropleth maps of crime count and rate, a bar chart of crime by type, and three socioeconomic choropleth maps.

Fourth, \textbf{Streamlit app}: It allows users to interactively explore crime patterns and their relationship to socioeconomic variables.

**Weaknesses and difficulties encountered:**

- \textbf{Temporal scope}: The crime dataset spans 2001–present but was filtered to 2024 only due to file size constraints, precluding any time-series or trend analysis across years.
- \textbf{Spatial join precision}: An initial spatial join using `intersects` without first clipping tracts to the Chicago city boundary inadvertently included partial Cook County tracts and water bodies such as Lake Michigan. This was resolved by first clipping all Illinois tracts to the Chicago city boundary shapefile, then using a `within` predicate to assign each crime point to exactly one tract.
- \textbf{Correlation, not causation}: The socioeconomic analysis identifies associations between tract-level conditions and crime rates but cannot establish causal relationships.

```{python}
#| include: false
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import altair as alt
import json

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
alt.renderers.enable("png")
alt.data_transformers.disable_max_rows()

# improve graph Resolution
import tempfile
from IPython.display import SVG, display, Image
import vl_convert as vlc

def display_altair_png(chart, scale=2):
    png_bytes = vlc.vegalite_to_png(chart.to_dict(), scale=scale)

    # Write to a temporary PNG file and display
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".png") as tmp:
        tmp.write(png_bytes)
        tmp.flush()
        display(Image(filename=tmp.name))
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Load data
DATA_DIR = Path("../data")

# Crime
crime_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "Crimes_-_2001_to_Present_20260304.csv")
print("Crime loaded:", crime_raw.shape)

# Education
edu_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "ACSDT5Y2024.B15003-Data.csv")
print("Education loaded:", edu_raw.shape)

# Unemployment rate
unemp_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "ACSST5Y2024.S2301-Data.csv")
print("Unemployment loaded:", unemp_raw.shape)

# Income
income_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "ACSDT5Y2024.B19013-Data.csv")
print("Income loaded:", income_raw.shape)

# Population
pop_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "ACSDT5Y2024.B01003-Data.csv")
print("Population loaded:", pop_raw.shape)
pop_raw.head()

# Tract shapefile
tract_raw = gpd.read_file(DATA_DIR / "tl_2023_17_tract" / "tl_2023_17_tract.shp")
print("Tract loaded:", tract_raw.shape)
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Clean crime data
crime = crime_raw[[
    "Date",
    "Primary Type",
    "Year",
    "Latitude",
    "Longitude"
]].copy()

# Set 31 "Primary Type" to 4 types
violent = {
    "HOMICIDE",
    "BATTERY",
    "ASSAULT",
    "ROBBERY",
    "CRIMINAL SEXUAL ASSAULT",
    "SEX OFFENSE",
    "KIDNAPPING",
    "STALKING",
    "INTIMIDATION"
}

property_crime = {
    "THEFT",
    "BURGLARY",
    "MOTOR VEHICLE THEFT",
    "CRIMINAL DAMAGE",
    "CRIMINAL TRESPASS",
    "ARSON",
    "DECEPTIVE PRACTICE"
}

regulatory = {
    "NARCOTICS",
    "OTHER NARCOTIC VIOLATION",
    "LIQUOR LAW VIOLATION",
    "WEAPONS VIOLATION",
    "CONCEALED CARRY LICENSE VIOLATION",
    "GAMBLING",
    "PROSTITUTION",
    "PUBLIC INDECENCY",
    "OBSCENITY",
    "HUMAN TRAFFICKING"
}

other = {
    "OTHER OFFENSE",
    "OFFENSE INVOLVING CHILDREN",
    "INTERFERENCE WITH PUBLIC OFFICER",
    "PUBLIC PEACE VIOLATION",
    "NON-CRIMINAL"
}

# Function to classify crime
def classify_crime(x):
    if x in violent:
        return "Violent"
    elif x in property_crime:
        return "Property"
    elif x in regulatory:
        return "Regulatory"
    else:
        return "Other"

# Create new column and drop "Primary Type" column
crime["crime_type"] = crime["Primary Type"].apply(classify_crime)

crime = crime.drop(columns="Primary Type")

print("Crime cleaned:", crime.shape)
display(crime.head())
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Clean education data
edu = edu_raw.iloc[3:][[
    "GEO_ID",
    "B15003_001E",
    "B15003_021E",
    "B15003_022E",
    "B15003_023E",
    "B15003_024E"
]].copy()

# Extract tract_id
edu["tract_id"] = edu["GEO_ID"].str[-11:]

# Convert to numeric
cols = [
    "B15003_001E",
    "B15003_021E",
    "B15003_022E",
    "B15003_023E",
    "B15003_024E"
]

edu[cols] = edu[cols].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce")

# Bachelor+ population
edu["bachelor_plus"] = (
    edu["B15003_021E"] +
    edu["B15003_022E"] +
    edu["B15003_023E"] +
    edu["B15003_024E"]
)

# Bachelor+ rate
edu["edu_rate"] = 100 * edu["bachelor_plus"] / edu["B15003_001E"]

# Keep only needed columns
edu = edu[["tract_id", "edu_rate"]]

print("Education cleaned:", edu.shape)
display(edu.head())
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Clean unemployment data
unemp = unemp_raw.iloc[3:][[
    "GEO_ID",
    "S2301_C04_001E"
]].copy()

# Extract tract id
unemp["tract_id"] = unemp["GEO_ID"].str[-11:]

# Convert to numeric
unemp["S2301_C04_001E"] = pd.to_numeric(
    unemp["S2301_C04_001E"],
    errors="coerce"
)

# Rename column
unemp = unemp.rename(
    columns={"S2301_C04_001E": "unemployment_rate"}
)

# Keep final columns
unemp = unemp[[
    "tract_id",
    "unemployment_rate"
]]

print("Unemployment cleaned:", unemp.shape)
display(unemp.head())
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Clean income data
income = income_raw.iloc[3:][[
    "GEO_ID",
    "B19013_001E"
]].copy()

# Extract tract id
income["tract_id"] = income["GEO_ID"].str[-11:]

# Convert to numeric
income["B19013_001E"] = pd.to_numeric(
    income["B19013_001E"],
    errors="coerce"
)

# Rename column
income = income.rename(
    columns={"B19013_001E": "median_income"}
)

# Keep final columns
income = income[[
    "tract_id",
    "median_income"
]]

print("Income cleaned:", income.shape)
display(income.head())
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Clean population data
pop = pop_raw.iloc[3:][[
    "GEO_ID",
    "B01003_001E"
]].copy()

# Extract tract id
pop["tract_id"] = pop["GEO_ID"].str[-11:]

# Convert to numeric
pop["B01003_001E"] = pd.to_numeric(
    pop["B01003_001E"],
    errors="coerce"
)

# Rename column
pop = pop.rename(
    columns={"B01003_001E": "population"}
)

# Keep final columns
pop = pop[[
    "tract_id",
    "population"
]]

print("Population cleaned:", pop.shape)
display(pop.head())
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Tracts Geodata: keep only Chicago tract IDs 
tracts = gpd.read_file(DATA_DIR / "tl_2023_17_tract" / "tl_2023_17_tract.shp")
chicago = gpd.read_file(DATA_DIR / "Boundaries - City_20260303" / "geo_export_06d21f1c-6fda-4eae-9de6-a54e6cd59005.shp")

# Align CRS
chicago = chicago.to_crs(tracts.crs)

# Keep tracts that intersect Chicago boundary (without cutting geometry)
tracts_chi = gpd.sjoin(
    tracts,
    chicago[["geometry"]],
    how="inner",
    predicate="intersects"
).drop(columns=["index_right"])

# Clip tracts to Chicago boundary
tracts_chi = gpd.clip(tracts_chi, chicago)

# Ensure tract id format
tracts_chi["GEOID"] = tracts_chi["GEOID"].astype(str).str.zfill(11)

# Chicago tract id set
chi_tract_ids = set(tracts_chi["GEOID"])
print(f"Total tracts in Chicago: {len(chi_tract_ids)}")
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Crime Geodata: add tract_id column
crime_geo = crime.dropna(subset=["Latitude", "Longitude"]).copy()

crime_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    crime_geo,
    geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(crime_geo["Longitude"], crime_geo["Latitude"]),
    crs="EPSG:4326"
)

tracts_chi_4326 = tracts_chi.to_crs("EPSG:4326")

crime_tract = gpd.sjoin(
    crime_gdf,
    tracts_chi_4326[["GEOID", "geometry"]],
    how="inner",
    predicate="within"
).rename(columns={"GEOID": "tract_id"})

crime_tract["tract_id"] = crime_tract["tract_id"].astype(str).str.zfill(11)

print("Crime in Chicago: ", crime_tract.shape)
display(crime_tract.head())
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Education Geodata: keep only Chicago tracts
edu_gdf = tracts_chi.merge(
    edu,
    left_on="GEOID",
    right_on="tract_id",
    how="left"
)

edu_gdf.head()
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Unemployment Geodata: keep only Chicago tracts
unemp_gdf = tracts_chi.merge(
    unemp,
    left_on="GEOID",
    right_on="tract_id",
    how="left"
)

unemp_gdf.head()
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Income Geodata: keep only Chicago tracts
income_gdf = tracts_chi.merge(
    income,
    left_on="GEOID",
    right_on="tract_id",
    how="left"
)

income_gdf.head()
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Population Geodata: keep only Chicago tracts
pop_gdf = tracts_chi.merge(
    pop,
    left_on="GEOID",
    right_on="tract_id",
    how="left"
)

pop_gdf.head()
```
```{python}
#| include: false
# Merge Crime and ACS variables into tract GeoDataFrame
crime_by_tract = (
    crime_tract
    .groupby("tract_id", as_index=False)
    .size()
    .rename(columns={"size": "crime_count"})
)

# Merge crime + ACS into Chicago tracts GeoDataFrame
tracts_analysis = (
    tracts_chi
    .merge(pop, left_on="GEOID", right_on="tract_id", how="left")
    .drop(columns=["tract_id"])
    .merge(crime_by_tract, left_on="GEOID", right_on="tract_id", how="left")
    .drop(columns=["tract_id"])
    .merge(edu, left_on="GEOID", right_on="tract_id", how="left").drop(columns=["tract_id"])
    .merge(unemp, left_on="GEOID", right_on="tract_id", how="left").drop(columns=["tract_id"])
    .merge(income, left_on="GEOID", right_on="tract_id", how="left").drop(columns=["tract_id"])
)

# Fill tracts with no crimes + compute rate (per 1000 residents)
tracts_analysis["crime_count"] = tracts_analysis["crime_count"].fillna(0).astype(int)
tracts_analysis["crime_rate"] = (tracts_analysis["crime_count"] / tracts_analysis["population"]) * 100

tracts_analysis.head()
```

**Plots 1 & 2 — Crime Count by Month/Date and Month/Hour**

The heatmaps reveal clear temporal structures. Plot 1 (Month × Date) shows higher crime in summer months and on the 1st of each month. Plot 2 (Month × Hour) shows an evening peak (6–9 PM), especially in summer. Early-morning hours (2–5 AM) are uniformly low.

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Static plot 1: Crime count by Month and Date
crime_time = crime_tract.copy()
crime_time["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(crime_time["Date"], errors="coerce")

crime_time["month"] = crime_time["Date"].dt.month
crime_time["date"] = crime_time["Date"].dt.day

# Aggregate crime count
crime_calendar = (
    crime_time
    .groupby(["month", "date"], as_index=False)
    .size()
    .rename(columns={"size": "crime_count"})
)

# Month labels
month_order = [
    "Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun","Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"
]

crime_calendar["month_name"] = crime_calendar["month"].apply(lambda x: month_order[x-1])

# Heatmap
month_date_heatmap = (
    alt.Chart(crime_calendar)
    .mark_rect()
    .encode(
        x=alt.X(
            "month_name:N",
            title="Month",
            sort=month_order
        ),
        y=alt.Y(
            "date:O",
            title="Date"
        ),
        color=alt.Color(
            "crime_count:Q",
            title="Crime Count",
            scale=alt.Scale(scheme="redyellowblue", reverse=True)
        ),
        tooltip=[
            alt.Tooltip("month_name:N", title="Month"),
            alt.Tooltip("date:O", title="Date"),
            alt.Tooltip("crime_count:Q", title="Crime Count")
        ]
    )
    .properties(
        width=120,
        height=200,
        title="Crime Count by Month and Date"
    )
)

# Static plot 2: Crime count by Month and Hour
crime_time["hour"] = crime_time["Date"].dt.hour

# Aggregate counts
crime_month_hour = (
    crime_time
    .groupby(["month", "hour"], as_index=False)
    .size()
    .rename(columns={"size": "crime_count"})
)

# Month labels
crime_month_hour["month_name"] = crime_month_hour["month"].apply(lambda x: month_order[x-1])

# Heatmap
month_hour_heatmap = (
    alt.Chart(crime_month_hour)
    .mark_rect()
    .encode(
        x=alt.X("month_name:N", title="Month", sort=month_order),
        y=alt.Y("hour:O", title="Hour of Day"),
        color=alt.Color(
            "crime_count:Q",
            title="Crime Count",
            scale=alt.Scale(scheme="redyellowblue", reverse=True)
        ),
        tooltip=[
            alt.Tooltip("month_name:N", title="Month"),
            alt.Tooltip("hour:O", title="Hour"),
            alt.Tooltip("crime_count:Q", title="Crime Count")
        ]
    )
    .properties(
        width=120,
        height=200,
        title="Crime Count by Month and Hour"
    )
)

combined = alt.hconcat(
    month_date_heatmap,
    month_hour_heatmap
).resolve_scale(
    color="independent"
)

display_altair_png(combined, scale=2)
```
Following feedback from the presentation, we investigated several apparent anomalies:

- \textbf{Elevated counts on the 1st of each month and at 12:00 AM / 12:00 PM}: Minute- and second-level analysis confirms these are default placeholder values, with exact 00:00:00 timestamps account for 9.70% of records on the 1st versus 3.31% on other dates, and minute=0 is similarly over-represented at hours 0 and 12.

- \textbf{Low counts on January 14 and November 28}: Record counts are consistent between raw and cleaned data on both dates, suggesting the reductions are genuine and may attributable to reduced activity on federal holidays (MLK Day and Thanksgiving).

- \textbf{Low count on May 14}: The raw dataset contains 728 records for May 14, but only 114 remain after cleaning. Investigation reveals that 84% of May 14 records are missing geographic coordinates (citywide average completeness is 99.4%)and May 15 is the second-worst date by the same metric. We attribute this to a likely CPD system outage on those two days that prevented coordinate recording, rather than a true drop in crime.
```{python}
#| include: false
#| fig-align: center

# Investigate anormalies shown in the plots
# Create a check dataframe
crime_raw_check = crime_raw.copy()
crime_raw_check["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(crime_raw_check["Date"], errors="coerce")
crime_raw_check["month"] = crime_raw_check["Date"].dt.month
crime_raw_check["date"] = crime_raw_check["Date"].dt.day
crime_raw_check["hour"] = crime_raw_check["Date"].dt.hour
crime_raw_check["minute"] = crime_raw_check["Date"].dt.minute
crime_raw_check["second"] = crime_raw_check["Date"].dt.second
crime_raw_check["has_coords"] = (
    crime_raw_check["Latitude"].notna() & 
    crime_raw_check["Longitude"].notna()
)

# 1. Date Anomaly Checks

# 1.1 Jan 14 and Nov 28
for month, day in [(1, 14), (11, 28)]:
    raw = crime_raw_check[
        (crime_raw_check["month"] == month) &
        (crime_raw_check["date"] == day)
    ]
    surrounding = crime_raw_check[
        (crime_raw_check["month"] == month) &
        (crime_raw_check["date"].isin(range(day-3, day+4))) &
        (crime_raw_check["date"] != day)
    ]
    avg_surrounding = len(surrounding) / 6
    print(f"\n {month}/{day}")
    print(f"Total records on this day: {len(raw)}")
    print(f"Avg records on surrounding 3 days: {avg_surrounding:.0f}")
    print(f"Coordinate completeness rate: {raw['has_coords'].mean():.2%}")

# 1.2 May 14
print("May 14")
may14_raw = crime_raw_check[
    (crime_raw_check["month"] == 5) &
    (crime_raw_check["date"] == 14)
]
print(f"\n Total records in raw data: {len(may14_raw)}")
print(f"Coordinate completeness rate: {may14_raw['has_coords'].mean():.2%}")
print(f"Citywide avg completeness rate: {crime_raw_check['has_coords'].mean():.2%}")

# Bottom 5 dates by coordinate completeness
all_completeness = (
    crime_raw_check
    .groupby(["month", "date"])["has_coords"]
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"has_coords": "completeness_rate"})
    .sort_values("completeness_rate")
)
print(f"\n Bottom 5 dates by coordinate completeness: \n {all_completeness.head().to_string(index=False)}")

# Crime type distribution of missing-coordinate records (ruling out duplicates)
may14_missing = may14_raw[~may14_raw["has_coords"]]
print(f"\n  Records missing coordinates: {len(may14_missing)}")
print(f"Crime type distribution (missing coords): \n {may14_missing["Primary Type"].value_counts().head(10).to_string()}")

# 1.3 1st of Each Month
print("\n 1st of Each Month")
day1 = crime_raw_check[crime_raw_check["date"] == 1]
other_days = crime_raw_check[crime_raw_check["date"] != 1]

midnight = lambda df: ((df["hour"]==0) & (df["minute"]==0) & (df["second"]==0)).mean()
print(f"Share of 00:00:00 on the 1st: {midnight(day1):.2%}")
print(f"Share of 00:00:00 on other days: {midnight(other_days):.2%}")
print(f"Share of hour=0 on the 1st: {(day1['hour']==0).mean():.2%}")
print(f"Share of hour=0 on other days: {(other_days['hour']==0).mean():.2%}")

# 2. Time Anomaly Checks

# 2.1 Midnight & Noon Spike
print("\n Midnight & Noon Spike")
h0_h12 = crime_raw_check[crime_raw_check["hour"].isin([0, 12])]
other_hours = crime_raw_check[~crime_raw_check["hour"].isin([0, 12])]

exact_zero = lambda df: ((df["minute"]==0) & (df["second"]==0)).mean()
print(f"Share of xx:00:00 at hour 0 & 12: {exact_zero(h0_h12):.2%}")
print(f"Share of xx:00:00 at other hours: {exact_zero(other_hours):.2%}")

# Minute distribution comparison
print("\n  Minute distribution at hour 0 & 12:")
print(h0_h12["minute"].value_counts().sort_index().head(5).to_string())
print("\n  Minute distribution at other hours (first 5 minutes):")
print(other_hours["minute"].value_counts().sort_index().head(5).to_string())
```

**Plots 3 & 4 — Crime Count and Crime Rate Choropleth Maps**

Plot 3 maps raw crime counts, where the Loop and Near North Side dominate due to high commercial and transit activity, making spatial variation elsewhere difficult to discern. Plot 4 adjusts for population (crime rate per 100 residents), producing a more evenly distributed and informative picture that highlights elevated risk in South and West Side tracts that would otherwise appear moderate in absolute terms.

```{python}
#| fig-align: center
#| echo: false

# Static plot 3: Crime Count Choropleth Map
tracts_plot = tracts_analysis.to_crs("EPSG:4326").copy()
geojson_dict = json.loads(tracts_plot.to_json())

crime_count_map = (
    alt.Chart(
        alt.InlineData(
            values=geojson_dict,
            format=alt.DataFormat(type="json", property="features")
        )
    )
    .mark_geoshape(stroke="white", strokeWidth=0.2)
    .encode(
        color=alt.Color(
            "properties.crime_count:Q",
            title="Crime Count",
            scale=alt.Scale(scheme="orangered")
        ),
        tooltip=[
            alt.Tooltip("properties.GEOID:N", title="Tract"),
            alt.Tooltip("properties.crime_count:Q", title="Crime count"),
        ],
    )
    .properties(
        width=200,
        height=200,
        title="Chicago Crime Count by Census Tract (2024)"
    )
    .project(type="mercator")
)

# Static plot 4: Crime Rate Choropleth Map
tracts_plot = tracts_analysis.to_crs("EPSG:4326").copy()
geojson_dict = json.loads(tracts_plot.to_json())

crime_rate_map = (
    alt.Chart(
        alt.InlineData(
            values=geojson_dict,
            format=alt.DataFormat(type="json", property="features")
        )
    )
    .mark_geoshape(stroke="white", strokeWidth=0.2)
    .encode(
        color=alt.Color(
            "properties.crime_rate:Q",
            title="Crime Rate",
            scale=alt.Scale(scheme="orangered")
        ),
        tooltip=[
            alt.Tooltip("properties.GEOID:N", title="Tract"),
            alt.Tooltip("properties.crime_count:Q", title="Crime count"),
            alt.Tooltip("properties.population:Q", title="Population"),
            alt.Tooltip("properties.crime_rate:Q", title="Crime rate", format=".2f"),
        ],
    )
    .properties(
        width=200,
        height=200,
        title="Chicago Crime Rate by Census Tract (2024)"
    )
    .project(type="mercator")
)

combined = alt.hconcat(
    crime_count_map, crime_rate_map
).resolve_scale(
    color="independent"
)

display_altair_png(combined, scale=2)
#display_altair_png(crime_count_map | crime_rate_map, scale=2)
```

**Plot 5 — Crime Count by Type**

Property crime dominates (~140,000 incidents), followed by Violent (~80,000), Other (~22,000), and Regulatory (~18,000). The prevalence of economically motivated property crime suggests it is more likely to co-vary with income and unemployment, motivating the socioeconomic analysis in Part 2.

```{python}
#| fig-align: center
# Aggregate crime counts by grouped crime category
crime_type_counts = (
    crime_tract
    .groupby("crime_type", as_index=False)
    .size()
    .rename(columns={"size": "crime_count"})
)

# Create bar chart of crime counts by type
crime_type_bar = (
    alt.Chart(crime_type_counts)
    .mark_bar()
    .encode(
        x=alt.X("crime_type:N", title="Crime Category", sort="-y"),
        y=alt.Y("crime_count:Q", title="Crime Count"),
        color=alt.Color(
            "crime_count:Q",
            scale=alt.Scale(scheme="orangered"),
            legend=None
        ),
        tooltip=[
            "crime_type:N",
            "crime_count:Q"
        ]
    )
    .properties(
        width=200,
        height=200,
        title="Crime Count by Type"
    )
)

crime_type_bar
```

**Plots 6, 7 & 8 — Socioeconomic Choropleth Maps**

These three maps display the spatial distribution of education rate, unemployment rate, and median income across Chicago census tracts. All three exhibit geographic patterns strikingly similar to the crime rate map (Plot 4), with socioeconomic disadvantage concentrated in the same South and West Side tracts that bear the highest crime burden,which motivates the correlation analysis in the Streamlit app.

```{python}
#| fig-align: center
# Set base map
tracts_plot = tracts_analysis.to_crs("EPSG:4326").copy()
geojson_dict = json.loads(tracts_plot.to_json())

base = (
    alt.Chart(
        alt.InlineData(
            values=geojson_dict,
            format=alt.DataFormat(type="json", property="features")
        )
    )
    .mark_geoshape(stroke="white", strokeWidth=0.2)
    .project(type="mercator")
    .properties(width=350, height=450)
)

# Education choropleth
edu_map = base.encode(
    color=alt.Color(
        "properties.edu_rate:Q",
        title="Education (%)",
        scale=alt.Scale(scheme="greens", type="quantile")
    ),
    tooltip=[
        alt.Tooltip("properties.GEOID:N", title="Tract"),
        alt.Tooltip("properties.edu_rate:Q", title="Education (%)", format=".1f")
    ]
).properties(title="Education Rate by Tract")

# Unemployment choropleth
unemp_map = base.encode(
    color=alt.Color(
        "properties.unemployment_rate:Q",
        title="Unemployment (%)",
        scale=alt.Scale(scheme="blues", type="quantile")
    ),
    tooltip=[
        alt.Tooltip("properties.GEOID:N", title="Tract"),
        alt.Tooltip("properties.unemployment_rate:Q", title="Unemployment (%)", format=".1f")
    ]
).properties(title="Unemployment Rate by Tract")

# Income choropleth
income_map = base.encode(
    color=alt.Color(
        "properties.median_income:Q",
        title="Median Income (USD)",
        scale=alt.Scale(scheme="purples", type="quantile")
    ),
    tooltip=[
        alt.Tooltip("properties.GEOID:N", title="Tract"),
        alt.Tooltip("properties.median_income:Q", title="Median Income", format=",.0f")
    ]
).properties(title="Median Income by Tract")

combined = alt.hconcat(
    edu_map, unemp_map, income_map
).resolve_scale(
    color="independent"
)

display_altair_png(combined, scale=2)
# display_altair_png(edu_map | unemp_map | income_map, scale=2)
```
